title: Japanese Vegetable Tempura

subs.name: any firm vegetable — sweet potato, baby corn, sliced onion rings or pumpkin all fry beautifully in tempura batter

ingredients.name: mushrooms (shiitake or button), whole or halved

subs.name: rice flour (chawal ka atta) for a gluten-free batter that is even lighter and more delicate
subs.notes: available at any Indian grocery store

ingredients.name: cornflour (cornstarch)
visual_checks: key to a light, shattering crust
ingredients.notes: key to a light, shattering crust

subs.name: potato starch in the same quantity for a very similar effect
replace with an extra 30 ml of ice water for a vegan batter that is slightly lighter and less golden

ingredients.name: ice-cold water
visual_checks: place a bowl of water over ice; cold temperature is non-negotiable for tempura batter
ingredients.notes: place a bowl of water over ice; cold temperature is non-negotiable for tempura batter

ingredients.name: oil (for deep frying)
visual_checks: neutral oil such as sunflower or refined groundnut
ingredients.notes: neutral oil such as sunflower or refined groundnut

ingredients.name: dashi or light vegetable stock (for dipping sauce)

steps.text: Pat all vegetables completely dry with kitchen paper — surface moisture creates steam and prevents the batter from crisping. Cut everything to roughly uniform thickness so pieces cook in similar time. Lay out on a tray ready to coat.
Make the dipping sauce first: combine soy sauce, stock and sugar in a small saucepan, stir over low heat for 1 minute until sugar dissolves. Remove from heat and cool to room temperature.

Make the batter immediately before frying: crack the cold egg into a bowl, add the ice water and stir briefly. Tip in flour and cornflour all at once and mix with a fork using a folding motion — no more than 10 strokes. Visible lumps are correct; overmixing develops gluten and makes the batter heavy.

Heat oil in a kadai or deep saucepan to 170 to 175 degrees C. Test with a drop of batter — it should sink slightly, rise immediately and sizzle actively. Fry in small batches to maintain temperature; crowding cools the oil.

Dip each vegetable piece into the batter, letting excess drip off, and lower it gently into the oil. Fry for 2 to 3 minutes per batch, turning once, until the coating is pale gold and the sound changes from loud sizzling to a quieter crackle. Drain on a wire rack.

Between batches, skim the oil surface clean of loose batter crumbs which burn and bitter the oil. Serve tempura within 5 minutes of frying alongside the dipping sauce — tempura loses its crunch quickly and does not reheat well.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: egg → replace with an extra 30 ml of ice water for a vegan batter that is slightly lighter and less golden (also removes the egg allergen); maida → rice flour (chawal ka atta) for a gluten-free batter that is even lighter and more delicate (available at any Indian grocery store); cornflour → potato starch in the same quantity for a very similar effect; broccoli → any firm vegetable — sweet potato, baby corn, sliced onion rings or pumpkin all fry beautifully in tempura batter
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A deep kadai is the perfect vessel for tempura frying. Keep a kitchen thermometer handy; 170 degrees C is the target. Cornflour (cornstarch) is widely available at Indian grocery stores. The cold-batter discipline is the main technique to internalise — keep your mixing bowl over ice if possible. Serve with soy sauce as a dip if proper tsuyu (dashi-based) is not available.
